comment: I saw a rumor when this movie was first announced that The Rock was going to play agent Bubbles—— I am so glad they didn’t go that route. It’s great to see non-super celebrity faces in this movie!! Edit: For clarification, I’m not stating that all the actors are completely unknown or not famous in anyway, I’m simply saying that it’s nice to see people who feel like they were picked for the roles for reasons other than their fame. The reason we see so many movies with people like The Rock, Ryan Renalds, Jack Black, etc is primarily because it helps with advertising and these actors are paid no matter how good they act, often leading to low effort performances. This movie doesn’t feel like it’s throwing celebrities in our faces, even if there are some familiar faces in the cast. It gives me hope that it might actually be well thought out and well… good

title: Lilo & Stitch
comment: What I find so interesting is that the original director of the film also voiced Stitch. Now, for this live-action adaptation, he is not directing the film, but he has been asked to reprise his role as Stitch. Can you imagine how exciting yet potentially uncomfortable it must be to take direction, follow instructions, and read an adaptation of something you originally created and directed? On one hand, it is amazing to still be a part of something you helped bring to life. But on the other hand, it might feel strange to step back and watch someone else take the lead on a project that once was entirely yours.

title: Michael
comment: I was 20 yrs old and in JAIL for driving without a license when Michael passed. All the inmates and police officers were crying. His passing was especially hard for the older inmates who grew up watching Michael when he was little; they would hold each other and cry inconsolably. The tv channels we had access to were limited, but anytime they aired anything about Michael, that is what we watched. Normally, they vote on what to watch on the one tv in the day room, but that week there was no voting, it was Michael only. I was there for one week, but his music and the rerun interviews we watched got us all through. We danced together, cried together, and prayed for his family together. I’m sure everyone remembers where they were when they heard Michael Jackson passed away. Also, I’m from South Bend, IN not far from Gary, so they were all in there talking about Gary and the good ole days.

title: Mufasa: The Lion King
comment: What I find so amazing about these classic movies like The Lion King is how popular they remain long after they first came out! The Lion King came out 30 years ago and its popularity has only grown since then! It's hard to find people who don't recognize the scene of Rafiki holding up Simba or the phrase Hakuna Matata. Even people born long after The Lion King first came out are familiar with scenes and songs from the movie! Safe to say that anything associated with The Lion King is gonna be big!

comment: The main reason I'm going to see this movie, in addition to being a huge Lion King fan, is to honor James Earl Jones, the original voice of Mufasa, who sadly passed away earlier this year. That said, I do like how they're trying to show more emotive facial expressions on the characters, it's nice to see Billy Eichner and Seth Rogen as Timon and Pumbaa again, and Zazu...oh, Zazu, even before you had to look after Simba, I can tell you still went through so much crazy stuff all in the line of duty. I commend you, man!

title: Superman
comment: To people asking why they don't just use subtitles... Those obviously exist, but it would be like if your first language were English (which I'm assuming for most of you it is), but you're also very good at German, and are watching a movie in French, which you don't know at all. You obviously don't want to watch the movie in French with no subtitles, because you wouldn't understand it at all. So, your options then are to watch it with German subtitles (your second language), or English subtitles (your first language). You'd probably prefer the subtitles to be in your first language.

comment: Idk why people are complaining about the song choice without understanding the context of why they chose it and why Ethel Cain’s story works. The song actually fits the plot of this movie (which is based on the book) pretty well. The cheater Lily vowed not to be in an abusive relationship like her mother and father yet falls in love with a man who ends up abusing her and you see both the humanity and horror of the love story… it also lends an empathetic lens to women who find it hard to leave abusive relationships because they are often in love with the one that hurts them. It’s called It Ends With Us because it is a choice to end the cycle of abuse, before the abuser ends your livelihood.

comment: “The Mandalorian & Grogu” was absolutely fantastic. The scene where Din and Grogu parted ways in the forest felt just like watching the series finale, and it really tugged at my heartstrings. But I would have loved to see a scene where Sigourney Weaver and Din developed a bond of trust similar to the one between Carson Teva and Din. I think of this movie as “The Mandalorian & Grogu: Episode 1.” I don’t know if the sequel will be a streaming release or a movie, but ultimately, I want to see “everyone” come together to face the final battle. For the final shot, I’d love to see the parent and child relaxing at home, just like in Season 3. They could even reuse that exact scene—I wouldn’t mind at all.

title: Backrooms
comment: Thank you for making this incredible film! I was on the edge of my seat from start to finish, and there is no end of questions. Just my cup of tea. My most memorable experience: The movie had conditioned me to see in an analogue horror light throughout so when the credits cut off and screen went blank signaling the end of the film, I just sat watching the screen for what felt like 30 seconds, waiting for what was next. When nothing happened, I noticed that the theater was dead silent. I looked around and to my surprise, I was one of two people still left. I just got up slowly and left. My god, I felt like I'd open the theater door to enter another theater. I was just primed for it. The walk home was me unpacking the crazy master piece I just watched, where I continually looked around to ensure I didn't enter a backrooms of my own town. Kane Pixel, I always knew our tastes in film making and story telling were downright similar. I was smiling the entire time. The quiet moments spoke louder than any exposition so my brain had a fricking feast. What an experience! Smartest movie I saw in a long time because it genuinely respected my ability to read between the lines and draw my own conclusions. I will say that this movie felt like the first third of a three act story though. I will be down for another Backrooms movie made in the same style as this, but up the anty even more, like showcasing that red dimension from your own videos! I just love the idea of the therapist lady making an expedition so far into the backrooms that reality starts to break down even more than it already has. Think Interstellar's library 5th dimension or Inception's limbo or even that mall underground that was at one point actually a real place. Blend fiction into reality! Bottom line, I just want more! Your tastes regarding story telling and filmmaking were just chef's kiss. Please, continue your amazing work. I hope you get the box office earnings you genuinely deserve! First movie I'd seen on the big screen since Interstellar. Just enthrawling. Afternoon well spent

title: Marty Supreme
comment: I would never tell someone not to follow their passion! My parents NEVER told us that we “shouldn’t”. They gave us their experiences, gave us their advice, but never once do I remember them telling us “you can’t” or “you shouldn’t”. They also gave us the tools to cope with disappointment and I think that was one of the most important things either of them gave us. My father once told me that he couldn’t tell me what to do, but he asked me if I wanted to “look back and say I tried that…didn’t work” or “look back and say I wish I had, but never did”. Every decision was based on that question since.

comment: Memories! In 2003, I had a meltdown when my son got his lip pierced. The following weekend we patched things up by going to see Freaky Friday. title: 28 Years Later
comment: Using the Boots Poem instead of an official song is genius. The Boots Poem details how a soldier can be driven insane by the constant tone of boots marching, symbolism for how a person in the 28 Days Later universe can be driven insane by the constant screams, moans, fights/encounters of the infected. There’s a section in the 28 Weeks Later comic where a group of survivors in London uses a cage that’s constantly surrounded by the infected at night. There’s constant moans and screams of the infected can tear apart and drive even the most strong minded of people to the point of insanity.

comment: Ok, from what I can see from the new 28 Years Later trailer, there is this offshore island some survivors of the initial Rage Virus outbreak hid in, and this father and son (now old enough to hunt), are armed with Katniss-style bows are sent out to the mainland via a narrow bridge to scavenge stuff presumably on the UK mainland. They’ll be dealing with presumably any remaining human survivor presence in the UK, which are probably apocalyptic cultish tribes, and any lingering zombies that have somehow mutated and survived long enough to endure for 28 years. Additionally, NATO troops still has skin in the game, sending out troops to survey wasteland Britain, and maybe will encounter the survivors and either evacuate them or establish another military foothold on the island again, like 28 Weeks Later. Additionally, Alex Garland seems to have said that Paris was nuked, meaning Europe was saved from the zombies that managed to reach Mainland Europe, so we can assume that the old world was saved. 28 Years Later will be an epic movie, and they did what a trailer should do that is rarely even seen done correctly in these years, actually hide the highlights, leave you guessing and hyped for what’s in the movie.

title: TRON: Ares
comment: 0:42 Tron Lives I’ve been waiting for a day like this to come for almost 15 years, 15 years since my dad introduced me to the digital world of Tron when I was still a 7 years old kid. Tron legacy changed me life and my taste for cinema, with Special Effects that like its 80s predecessor was ahead of its time. And now it finally came for the return of Tron! Not in the way I expected it ever would, but better. This time, they came to us. Right off the bat for Ares was the music, oh my gosh; Nine Inch Nails was absolutely radical in this movie man. Personally I’ll never get over the amazing work Daft Punk left us with the soundtrack from Legacy, but NIN sure lives up to the standards for techno music in Tron. Each action sequence was paired perfectly with the sound and practically complemented each other throughout the movie. Especially during the grid chase scene on the speed boat, it was like watching those Music visuals that match with the sound. Then the Visuals and sets, continues to be the main background character of every Tron movie and just as good as the originals. Each shot had a story behind it as the film progressed, the use of color was more prominent I think in this one. Especially during the scenes where the programs were in our world this time. It was fascinating, there was almost an alien like aspect to their presence while at the same time feeling normal to see. Then the scenes that take place in grid from the 80s were an absolute nostalgia trip. Felt like they brought the original film back to life with a familiar appearance and afresh new coat of CGI paint Story wise, it was like watching a reverse Isekai film where characters you associate with the “other world” coming to the Mc’s world instead, no reincarnation lol. while their were times it felt rushed and had little plot points that tied to the last movie with Sam and Quorra, I found Ares to be a straightforward continuation of the franchise. There was stuff I wish was in it, but was still an exhilarating experience to watch. Especially during the final act, it had a tone similar to the finale scene of the first movie which had more emphasis of the conflict in a concluding depiction. As for characters, probably the most equally interesting cast that had ever been in the series. Each character you see had a unique contribution to the film, perhaps 1 mistake the first 2 made. Ares as the main character was an interesting journey from start to finish. Can’t remember the last time watching the beginnings of a hero who started off as a servant of evil. He was even funny a couple times with good one liners. I’d say this was Jared Leto’s breakout role after the mixed portrayals in suicide squad and morbius, only with Wallace from Blade Runner being equal in performance. Overall, Tron Ares was well worth the wait. It evokes the best that both Tron and Tron Legacy had to offer their audiences and returning fans wanted to see. Highly recommend watching in IMAX to get the full feeling of the experience. Solid 8/10 for me.

comment: Fun fact: Based upon the book "Mickey7" by Edward Ashton, Mickey died approximately 6 times. The film however, states he died 16 times. Meaning that Mickey Barnes' film-counterpart suffered far more than his original part with death.

title: Mickey 17
comment: Fun fact: This movie was green lit before the book was actually written. Bong Joon Ho wanted to do the movie based on the outline for the book alone. That's why the book has 7 and the film has 17 Mickeys. Bong took some liberties when crafting his version. The author's agent pitched the story as a movie, unbeknownst to him, and studios were jumping at it.

comment: For those who don't know: Bong Joon Ho is one of the best directors in the world, straight up. He rivals Park Chan Wook as the best director working in South Korea. His best movie is Memories of Murder, but he's also made several others including The Host, Snowpiercer, Okja, and Parasite. His movies are known for being disturbing and representing the dark side of humanity, but this one seems like he's going for a dark comedy/satirical approach which could work really well. I would strongly recommend watching his earlier movies so you can understand his vibe.

It took me quite a few pages for my brain to process and understand the old English style of language, having no real substantial education, but you have a lot of time of in prison to read books, so I eventually ploughed through the a book of Complete works of Shakespeare which I found in the Prison library.....it was a great experience just immersing myself in his Great literary world of imagination. Id often just sit on the yard off to side reading my big book of Shakespeare while the others prisoners just walked round and round in circles which was the only form exercise allowed in the yard at that time, walking in circles. 2 years later I left prison and went straight to a local College to study English Literature, amongst other things, and the first Play we studied was Hamlet.....the other students were very surprised when they discovered I could recite Hamlets famous soliloquy and other passages by heart. So this film has better be bloody good, otherwise I'll be quite upset! But seems it is.....seems nailed on for several Oscars to! I'll bring back memories for me that's for sure. Hamlet was my first step back into Education, I related to Hamlet in a way I can't explain, and ultimately that simple curiosity of picking up a book of the shelf in a prison library to see what all the fuss was about with Shakespeare, eventually led me all the way to University. So I guess I have Shakespeare to thank, for not only has his works impacted and changed the World, he also changed my life too.....the power of words, language, and books, can never be underestimated. I hope this movie does Shakespeare and Hamlet justice, for Justice was not what I received. 🙏❤️

comment: A perfect film. Breathtaking in its intimacy. An authentic and raw meditation on love and grief. Humanizes Agnes Hathaway, a figure often relegated to the fringes of her famous husband's history, and William Shakespeare, a man more adept at expressing himself with the written word than with speaking (a problem I know too well). Of all the films I've been catching up on recently, this was the most emotionally cathartic.

comment: So this film is more than a man with a life style and ambition. This is about strength and loss, a man who only knew how to cope with death of a loved one in his own art. He made the character of hamlet “brave, strong and willing, plus come to terms with death” he needed to see this visually because he was grieving. Hats off to Shakespeare he done it beautifully as in this lives on for centuries and as we know as “normal” folk we are forgotten about down the line, he made him brave, strong, a hero and also someone who was willing to be brave for death. He needed this and Agnes finally understood. She was the main rock and grieved straight away. There is two types of grief and they went through both. beautiful acting and beautiful performance thank you.

comment: I've seen movies that my objective mind would put over this one in terms of acting, bias (I'm not familiar with any of the actors nor with the director), theme or pretty much anything, but dude, this was THE movie that showed me just how much you can just sit and ponder upon one piece of cinema and not get over the feeling it left you with. I've seen saddsr movies, more enraging movies, but yet still, there was just SOMETHING about it that stayed with me. In 30 years of life this is the first time I experience that deep and long ponder over a movie and the emotions it left me with, something that I have not escaped since I've first seen it a few weeks ago, and it's still in my head. I don't know, the mindset it caught me in, the fact that I saw it in a proper medium to clearly comprehend THE GORGEOUS camera work, I don't know what it was but I'm just gratefull that the stars alligned and I got to live this experience trough this gorgeous movie.

comment: I quite enjoyed the Accountant and decided I would make a rare (these days) trip to the theater to see this sequel. Sadly, it fell into the category of sequels that were significantly worse than the original. Affleck's character was still quirky, and impressive, but not as fresh as before. They expanded on the role of his backup, the childhood friend who now operated some kind of all powerful cyber network. His brother was somewhat fun to watch, as the two teamed up quite a bit and they had some good byplay. But, overall, the movie felt disjointed, a bit hard to follow, and worst of all, a little boring. I would not recommend this to fans of the original.

title: Kantara - A Legend: Chapter 1
comment: Just watched Kantara today — what a masterpiece! A film that beautifully portrays our divine culture, humanity, and the true essence of Sanatana Dharma. It’s a cinematic celebration of the rich traditions and values that define our land. The cinematography is breathtaking, the music divine — every beat gives goosebumps and fills you with strength. The story is soulful, and the acting by Divine Star Rishab ✨ Shetty is truly phenomenal, especially his portrayal of Bhoota Kola, bringing alive the spirit and sanctity of our soil. A standing ovation to Hombale Films for delivering such a divine and powerful experience. Kudos to the entire team of Kantara — a film that touches the soul and glorifies our heritage. 🙏🔥 ಇಂದು ನಾನು ಕಾಂತಾರಾ ಸಿನಿಮಾ ನೋಡಿದೆ. ನಿಜವಾಗಿಯೂ ಅದ್ಭುತ ಸಿನಿಮಾ — ಇದು ನಮ್ಮ ದೈವಿಕ ಸಂಸ್ಕೃತಿ, ಮಾನವೀಯತೆ ಮತ್ತು ಧರ್ಮ ಎಂದರೆ ಏನು ಎಂಬುದನ್ನು ಹೇಳುವ ಚಿತ್ರ. ಇದು ನಿಜವಾದ ಸನಾತನ ಧರ್ಮದ ದೈವತ್ವವನ್ನು ತೋರಿಸುತ್ತದೆ. ನಾವು ಹಿಂದೂಗಳು ಅನುಸರಿಸುವ ಸಂಸ್ಕೃತಿಯ ಮಹತ್ವವನ್ನು ಅತ್ಯಂತ ಭಾವಪೂರ್ಣವಾಗಿ ಪ್ರದರ್ಶಿಸಿದೆ. ಛಾಯಾಗ್ರಹಣ ಅತ್ಯುನ್ನತ ಮಟ್ಟದಲ್ಲಿದೆ, ಸಂಗೀತ ದೈವಿಕ — ಮನಸ್ಸಿಗೆ ಕಂಪನ, ದೇಹಕ್ಕೆ ರೋಮಾಂಚನ ನೀಡುತ್ತದೆ. ಕಥೆ ಅಸಾಧಾರಣ. ಡಿವೈನ್ ಸ್ಟಾರ್ ರಿಷಭ್ ಶೆಟ್ಟಿ✨ ಅವರ ಅಭಿನಯ — ವಿಶೇಷವಾಗಿ ಅವರು ಚಿತ್ರದಲ್ಲಿ ತೋರಿಸಿದ ಭೂತಕೋಲಾ — ನಮ್ಮ ಮಣ್ಣಿನ ದೈವಿಕತೆಯನ್ನು, ಶ್ರೀಮಂತಿಕೆಯನ್ನು, ಶ್ರೇಷ್ಠತೆಯನ್ನು ಅಚ್ಚುಕಟ್ಟಾಗಿ ತೋರಿಸಿದೆ. ಹೊಂಬಾಳೆ ಫಿಲ್ಮ್ಸ್ ಮಾಡಿದ ಕೆಲಸ ಅಪ್ರತಿಮ. ಕಾಂತಾರಾ ನಿರ್ಮಾಪಕರಿಗೆ ಹೃದಯಪೂರ್ವಕ ಅಭಿನಂದನೆಗಳು — ನಮ್ಮ ಸಂಸ್ಕೃತಿ ಮತ್ತು ಧರ್ಮದ ಗೌರವವನ್ನು ಪ್ರಪಂಚಕ್ಕೆ ತೋರಿಸಿದ ಚಿತ್ರ ಇದು. 🙏🔥

comment: What makes M Night Shayalaman a great filmmaker is his ability to spin a concept or niche on its head. The unbreakable trilogy is a deconstruction of superheroes and super villains. With trap, the antagonist is the actual victim because he is trapped in an environment and in a situation where he has very little options to escape. Therefore he must do whatever it takes to get out. This approach to psychological thrillers is refreshing and smart. Depending on the execution, this has the potential to be one of his best films. I sincerely hope so.

comment: Theory: This movie Trap will be based within the Unbreakable universe and/or connected to the Unbreakable storyline. In the Unbreakable Trilogy, the main character in each movie is color coded with their own specific hue. The Overseer is always signified by the color green. Mr. Glass is associated with the color purple, and Kevin/The Beast can always be seen in and around the color yellow. This is solidified in the main poster for the movie "Glass"....So if you notice this guy, this main character in this movie "Trap" is seemingly synonymous with the color red. The red bathroom stalls, the red floor in the main lobby, the red stripe in his shirt, the red apron he puts on in one scene, the red cup he holds in another scene, the red tint and red concert lights, etc. plus the single word title "Trap" in the same vein of the "Unbreakable" trilogy makes me think we might be getting a full blown franchise

comment: Trailer is excellent! The movie starts out like ‘soft porn’, morphs into Sci-Fi, and ends as a gore/slasher flick. Negative feedback… too much unnecessary nudity/sexualization, a little over the top with the gore, and a bit longer than it needs to be to get the point across. title: The Count of Monte Cristo
comment: The Count of Monte Cristo is my favorite book !!! It is because I heard many things about the Count of Monte Cristo since he was a child. So, I decided to buy the book one day and read it all !!! After searching through many bookstores in my city, I found it and it was very difficult because after a couple of hours I realized that it was an out-of-print book. When I bought it, I was reading it on the bus on the way from my house to school and vice versa. I wasn't bored reading it in the least and it got interesting as the book progressed until I have finished it. Definitely, the best book that I've ever read 👍 My favorite parts are when Edmundo finds the treasure on the island of Monte Cristo and when he starts to reward the people who were loyal to him. Also when Monte Cristo reveals to Maximilian Morrel that he helped his father and family in the past. Consequently, Maximilian and his sister hugged Monte Cristo I have always wanted to see a production, whether TV or film, that perfectly expresses what is interpreted in the book. I really liked Jim Caviezel's movie, but it doesn't express the ending well because Monte Cristo stays with Haydee and not Mercedes. As I once heard, Hollywood prefers a happy ending; but that is not the point because there were also flaws in the end of the count's enemies In addition to other things such as the absence of important characters such as Haydee, Maximiliano Morrel and Villefort's daughter Danglars does not end up imprisoned for theft of merchandise as happens in the movie. Danglars was a banker and ends up bankrupt and without family Fernando was never Edmundo's best friend, and Edmundo does not kill him with a sword. The book tells that Fernando commits suicide after losing his wife, his son, and his social prestige. Villefort does not end up imprisoned for the death of his father, but rather he goes crazy due to so many recent emotions and regrets from the past. I have seen the trailer for this movie and it looks incredible because apparently it follows everything related to the book. I say this because characters that I did not see in previous productions such as Haydee and Maximilian, they appear in this trailer and besides the sets and recorded scenes are also impressive. My favorite scene of this trailer was when the Count of Monte Cristo fought several men at the same time. In advance, many congratulations for this enormous production that I'm pretty sure it's going get excited all the fans of the Count of Monte Cristo and make them very happy !!!

title: The Long Walk
comment: I'm Filipino. This movie reminds me of the tragic event that took place in the Philippines in April 1942, known as the Bataan Death March. Thousands of Filipino and American soldiers, who had already endured exhaustion, starvation, and battle, were forced by the Japanese army to march over 60 miles under unbearable conditions. Compared to this real historical atrocity, what the film portrays seems far less severe, as the true events were much harsher and more devastating.

comment: Loving the Pavlovian bell throughout the trailer. >The discovery of classical conditioning was accidental. While researching the digestion of dogs, Pavlov observed that the dogs' physical responses to food gradually changed. Initially, the dogs only salivated when the food was directly presented. However, he later noticed that the dogs began to salivate slightly before the food arrived in response to sounds consistently associated with feeding, such as the sound of the food cart. To test his theory, Pavlov conducted an experiment where he rang a bell shortly before presenting food to the dogs. Initially, the dogs did not salivate in response to the bell. However, as they learned to associate the sound of the bell with the arrival of food, they began to salivate at the sound alone.

comment: BRAVO to this! This was amazing. A24 rarely disappoints. They are redefining the horror genre with films that make you think. There is a time and a place for ax wielding maniacs, and then there is intellectual horror. These A24 films are never as literal as their trailers. This movie keeps you guessing and was very suspenseful. The first half is a little slow because you’re expecting it to be paced differently due to horror generally moves faster…BUT they are setting up the story and every detail and every piece of dialogue matters later. This gets in your head and in a good way. It challenges people to THINK. The two lead actresses were amazing and I've never cared for Hugh Grant movies, but he is deliciously evil in this. Very well written and highly recommended.

comment: I am a first generation American from a Polish family. My parents (separately) managed to flee from oppressive Communist Poland at the time in the 70s. My dad attended to Cooper Union and became an architect. And in the late 70s, early 80s he was hired and help build Trump Tower (he told me he was never really paid well by DT and he had his builders threaten over and over again to be deported back to Poland (which was during the Solidarity period) for being too slow or not doing a good job). My dad died in 2010 from cancer and he never knew that he helped build a skyscraper for a future president. This film feels too personal for me to watch - the immigrant/exiled life in America. Coming to America from traumatic experience in Central Europe, the unscrupulous business side of the US. And the history of the people who actually build this country. It hits a nerve but I will watch it.

comment: I think I read about 3 books on the Nuremberg trials over the last 30 years. I wonder how historically accurate, or "Hollywood" this film will be. If anyone is wondering who that man was yelling "Seig Heil!" was supposed to be, it was Rudolph Hess when he was being escorted down a hallway in the prison when Herman Goering was also being escorted in the opposite direction. When Hess got close to Goring, he did the Nazi salute. Goering looked bewildered and said nothing and knew Hess was nuts. The prison commandant was there also and chewed out Hess for saying that and it was not permitted. But he did not shove Hess against a wall! Matter of fact, there was no physical abuse allowed on any of the defendants.

comment: Nuremberg Tribunal Charter Art. 19. The Tribunal shall not be bound by technical rules of evidence. It shall adopt and apply to the greatest possible extent expeditious and non-technical procedure, and shall admit any evidence which it deems to have probative value. Art. 21. The Tribunal shall not require proof of facts of common knowledge but shall take judicial notice thereof. It shall also take judicial notice of official governmental documents and reports of the United Nations, including the acts and documents of the committees set up in the various Allied countries for the investigation of war crimes, and the records and findings of military or other Tribunals of any of the United Nations.

title: Warfare
comment: I walked out, right after the first IED hit and the squad had to collapse back into the building. I wasn't a Seal, i was a Sergeant Major in the Dutch Army, stationed in Uruzgan, Afghanistan. And i have seen war. I lived it, every time we went out of the gate. Every TIC, IED, VBIED, suicide bomber, is still what keeps me awake at night. So i walked out of the theatre. Not because i thought the movie sucked, but because it triggered a response, inside of me, i did not want people in the theatre to see. And even now, i'm crying, because of the men i lost, the wounded, forever scarred by war. I will return to the movie, but at home, where i am safe.

title: Warfare
comment: This is the most accurate war film I've ever seen. At the beginning I was giddy ready for an action flick, but by the end of it I was watching it as more of a documentary. Almost like it was filmed during the events. Everything was flawless. It really took me back. The uniforms, the way people behave under stress in combat, the SOPs, the lingo, the behavior, the little details like gear accountability, I mean everything was depicted just like real life. There was no soundtrack. There was no clear cut story. It was just an op gone wrong from start to finish. No nonsense or filler. This is not Blackhawk Down. This is not Saving Private Ryan. This is a very methodical movie that portrays how combat occurred during the GWOT down to the very minute details. Honestly this movie probably won't win any awards, but if you're interested in seeing what the war in Iraq is really like, go see this film. It's solid. I give it a solid 10/10. If you're a vet, go see this if you can handle it. This is a movie by vets for vets.

It’s not exciting or entertaining to watch; it’s pure pain, trauma, sadness, and hell. Like war. Garland did a great job. title: Warfare
comment: When I was a kid my father and I would watch "Combat" and it drove him nuts. He served in the Marines for 26 years, fought in WWII and Korea (Silver Stars and Purple Hearts). He would shout at the TV about how you wouldn't do this or shouldn't do that, same with "War" movies. I used to wish he'd not comment so much, I thought the guys filming these must have been combat veterans themselves, at least somebody on the set had been. Then I joined the Army and went off to Vietnam. When I came back after my first tour, he and I smoked a cigarette out on the lanai, drank a beer together and cried together. We cried because we were sad, and proud and because we were laughing at the funny shit that happens sometimes. It was the first time I ever saw my father cry, but then we laughed ourselves sick telling stories to each other. Each time I came home, my mother was a wreck and my father and I laughed and drank beer (never more than two). Now, I'm a lot older and I do the same thing. My wife tells me "if you don't like what they do in the movies, why watch them?" and she has a point. I've stopped watching war movies...and I'm okay. I see these "Trailers" and I can hear my father roaring "Cover fire, don't you assholes know how to lay down covering fire?".

title: Borderlands
comment: To the director Eli Roth and Joe Crombie and whoever else helped make these decisions, I want you to know, and to realize the gravity of this statement: This is your M Night Shyamalan's "Avatar: The Last Airbender". There will be a permanent stain on your portfolio forever. I would be utterly embarrassed to release this. I would be drinking my sorrows away and hoping time itself stops so the day it releases never comes. I would want to give you the benefit of the doubt and say it was a budget issue, but it's clear the only directing that was done here was directing money at bad, expensive actor choices that could have otherwise been used better. I always wondered with M Night's Avatar: "Surely he wasn't a fan of the series, right? He was forced to do this, right?" and this makes me think the same thing.

comment: First thought: a real romcom! Classic and funny and sweet and smart! Yes! 2nd thought: I always kinda hoped Kevin James had this side to him! Awesome! And he is way more attractive at 60 than… he’s ever been! Awesome, too! Lastly: I really, really miss Italy. Had the privilege of living in Rome for a while thanks to a weird, chaotic, totally unique and random job offer a couple lifetimes (read: decades) ago. And, hell, that feeling of longing and impermanence and warmth that I fell into there… That feeling never went away and once you’ve had the honor of being allowed to feel it? It aches in your chest forever, like a bruise, but that’s okay because the thought of losing it, forgetting the incredible calm that created the pain in its absence? That would be so much worse. I really miss Italy.

comment: I may actually take my jilted butt to see that. My fiancee took off like a bolt and left me a Dear John on FB Messenger 3 weeks after my brain swelled and I found out I had growths in my brain. I will keep the personal info on that but it caused memeory loss and years to recover. 3 weeks was all it took of me being down and not able to be my normal self. I needed help. That was 7 years ago and I havent dated since. Well once to this crazy older lady my family forced me to go on to help me get over my fiancee. That was fun...... But that was it. I quit dating but I swear, not joking, watching that trailer gave me the strangest feeling I havent had in forever. Hope. Crazy but I honestly felt my old stomped on heart come back to life for a minute. Thank you for making a movie for us guys. It means a lot. And I dont blame the fiancee. It was a world she wasnt prepared for. I had always been sick my whole life and told her stuff like that could happen to me. Ultimately I am thankful, for both our sakes, it happened before I married her. I am not throwing shade at her. God knew what He was doing.

comment: One of the best films I've seen in a long time! An extraordinary insight into the highs and lows of fame. From rags to riches this film shows the raw personal journey and self destruction of a man fighting to be free from internal pain. Such a powerful message of what goes on behind the scenes that others take for granted. I laughed a lot, cried a lot and sang along to his music, all whilst feeling so much admiration of watching a once broken boy fight through his downfalls and demons to becoming a Betterman. Having suffered with my own journey and experienced suicide, watching this film made me feel so proud of Robbie as the person he is, knowing that he's still here and fought with everything he had, thankfully having found the love from his incredible wife and beautiful family. He truly does deserve this 🫂

comment: Treat yourself film of the day - Better Man As someone who spent many Sunday evenings in a pub singing well out of tune to Robbie Williams hits, watching *"Better Man"* was like taking a heartfelt trip down memory lane. Directed by Michael Gracey the guy who made Wolverine the greatest show man on earth,the film beautifully captures the essence of Robbie's journey and the impact he had on a generation. From the very first scene, the film immerses us in the world of Robbie Williams, portraying his highs and lows with a balance that keeps us both entertained and emotionally invested. Minutes into the film, we see just how Robert became Robbie—a transformation that’s depicted with such perfect detail, without the uncomfortableness of over-the-top special effects. It’s a meticulous and authentic portrayal that draws you right in. For those of us who grew up listening to Robbie, the film’s portrayal of his struggles and successes feels deeply personal. It’s a reminder of the days when we all thought we could be rock stars, even if just for a moment, standing with a microphone in hand, belting out *“Angels”* or *“Let Me Entertain You.” in the pub or on a holiday. But what truly sets this film apart is the poignant speech Robbie gives at the end. It resonates on a profound level, encapsulating the wisdom that comes with experience. His words about magic and the importance of self-realization are incredibly moving. As he speaks, you can't help but reflect on your own journey and the moments of clarity that have shaped who you are today. In conclusion, *"Better Man"* isn’t just a film for Robbie Williams fans—it's a film for anyone who has ever chased a dream, faced adversity, and come out the other side with a deeper understanding of themselves. It’s a tribute to the power of resilience and the magic of self-discovery. If you haven't seen it yet, do yourself a favor and watch it—you're in for a nostalgic and inspiring experience.

comment: Imagine this! •First, you make a simple but still funny kids movie called "Mousehunt". •Then.....oh boy! You make "The ring", one of the best horror movies from the 2000's. •Then, you make the "Pirates of the caribbean" trilogy, one of the best film trilogies ever made. •Then, you make "Rango", one of the best animated films ever made. •after a good Disney western movie, you become a forgotten director. But then, boom! You come back from the death saying "i'm alive, b_tches!" With another great movie!, "Good luck, have fun, don't die". We love you, Gore Verbinski! Welcome back!.

title: Omniscient Reader: The Prophecy
comment: It's trash, full-on trash. Miscast leads and sloppy pacing gutted what made Omniscient Reader great. The show strips away the novel’s inner voice and moral complexity, compresses key arcs into checklist scenes, and replaces dry, dark humor with overwrought melodrama. Acting often feels exaggerated—like heavy-handed, theatrical performances with too much emotion—so characters come off as “acting” rather than living; supporting players who drive the story are sidelined or changed, leaving the stakes hollow. Disappointing adaptation of a brilliant source - and I actually like some of these actors/actresses 😫

title: The Wizard of the Kremlin
comment: Just a comment on performance, only as far as I can see from the trailer. I've seen Putin's long press conferences and lots of video, and though Jude Law has turned in some stellar performances, this one is very cartoonish and hasn't really captured the man. There's a maxim that an actor shouldn't "comment on character", but there seems to be lots of it here. Plus, the make-up makes him look more like a blond, angry Kevin Spacey. Putin would not be my "dream president", but I think the title should just be, "We Hate Putin and Russia". Less subtle, but more reflective of the intent I think. 😄

title: The Ugly Stepsister
comment: Just finished this movie. I really wanted to cry for Elvira. All of her faults and imperfections aside (tore agnes' dress, exposed agnes for her affair with the stableboy), she was sold a dream (whether by her mother, society, or that stupid book she was reading) that she never had a chance to achieve. She was made fun of, ridiculed, told she was a waste of talent, unattractive (so not true; her blue eyes alone is a head turner), so she broke her body, her nose, her eyes, swallowed tapeworm, lost her hair, chopped off her feet, all to be rejected by the prince, who wasn't even the least bit charming. But she didn't care. She was in love with him and only him, not his wealth (it's debatable, but her dream sequences only ever showed the prince, riding on a horseback, in a castle, in a forest, just being near her, but never with piles of gold or wealth, which made me believe that she really just wants the prince), while agnes took advantage of her own beauty to marry the same prince she doesn't even love to begin with. When Elvira was laying down on the floor after falling down the steps, breaking her face and body, looking at agnes as she leaves with the prince, and her last dream sequence with the prince looking at her bloodied face and broken body on the floor in disgust and walking away, the look on her face, the resignation, there was a slight smile of acceptance, but still sad, was truly heartbreaking. She just wanted the prince, but he didn't want her back. And that tapeworm scene just shows how brutal reality is for her. I'm so glad Alma is there to take care of her. I loved the part where they were both laughing while Alma is wheelbarrowing Elvira down the steps because her feet are completely gone. It shows that they were still able to laugh in the aftermath of such a physically and emotionally traumatic circumstance. This movie is the best Cinderella movie I've seen, and it's funny because it's not even about Cinderella. If the job is to help me understand and make me extremely sympathetic to the "ugly" stepsister through showing her point of view, then mission accomplished. Lea Myren did such an amazing job portraying Elvira I almost developed a parasocial relationship with her. I just wanted to give her a hug. She was trying so hard! 😭 Bravo.

title: The Ugly Stepsister
comment: I watched The Ugly Stepsister and wow, what a ride. It takes the Cinderella story we all know and flips it by focusing on one of the stepsisters. Instead of the classic fairy tale vibe from Perrault, or even the darker version by the Brothers Grimm, this film dives deep into obsession, pain, and the brutal pursuit of beauty. It’s raw, messy, and totally disturbing. I’m not easily shocked, but the body horror had me closing my eyes more than once. Still, it’s a bold take that hits hard with its message. Definitely not for everyone, but unforgettable.

title: Wake Up Dead Man: A Knives Out Mystery
comment: A formidable interpretation of the Christian message and ecclesiastical mission has just arrived in an unexpected genre. This Rian Johnson movie illuminates – quite literally in one scene – self-sacrifice, forgiveness, and absolution: the letting go of original sin, Augustinian superbia. While most critics will probably focus on the ingenious plot and cinematic flair, the detective’s revelation marks a most striking turn: at the decisive moment, Benoit Blanc comes to grasp the motivation of the genuine believer (specifically, the removal of evidence, which incidentally opposes mere rule-following to a higher ethos); he then chooses to sacrifice his own ego, thereby transcending his former self. Unaware of it, it is his Christ-like act that allows the original sin of pride to be locked away on the cross by the ultimate self-sacrifice, the Lamb of God. A wonderful film.

title: The Death of Robin Hood
comment: When I was a kid, I had a Robin Hood storybook that had beautifully painted artwork. At the end, when Robin died, he was an old man and Marion had passed away already. Little John was the last of his friends who stayed by his bedside, and before he died, Robin asked him to help him sit up so he could use his bow one last time. He took his bow, and fired a single shot out of the window of his room before dying in John's grasp. John took him, and buried him where the arrow landed at Robin's request.

title: Mother Mary
comment: To everyone fixating on Anne’s aging, graceful or otherwise: She had a facelift. Which is FINE. We don’t actually owe anyone “graceful” aging, and saying women must age gracefully is just a softer way to say “get out of the way, hag!” The pressure to age “gracefully” or “like wine” seems like it comes from a positive place, but it’s actually about making sure that “disposable” women (which the internet tends to define as a woman who is close to or entering middle age) are easily identifiable so that they can be discarded, ignored, and denied opportunities. In so much of the world, women are told they’re too young to be taken seriously right up until the moment they’re told they’re too old to matter, and refusing to be easily identifiable by age is a huge act of power. I’m the same age as Anne, and when we were teenagers, women our current age were largely invisible in serious films unless they were a background mom or caregiving character. The fact that Anne is currently just coming into her power and prowess as an actress is a testament to the rise of generations of women who are not going to be classified according to the arbitrary expectations of tradition.

title: I Am Frankelda
comment: So I saw this film at the Manchester Animation Festival in 2025 and honestly it is absolutely stunning! I honestly think my jaw was dropped throughout most of the movie at the amount of detail in every single frame. The whole movie is beautiful, handmade, bright and colourful! After constantly seeing stupid AI ‘art’ everywhere, it is truly wonderful to see so many people’s talents coming to life on screen. The puppets have a so much character and the sets and props are each a work of art of their own! The story is so exciting and engaging too! You go on a fantastic journey seeing the characters grow and overcome challenges, with plenty of drama and comedy throughout (I don’t want to give too much of the plot away!) 5/5 stars would watch again and again for sure!

title: Iron Maiden: Burning Ambition
comment: I bought the first Iron Maiden when I was a kid when it was the only Iron Maiden album. The cover grabbed my attention in a record store. I was all-in, and I never looked back. First saw them live with Di'Anno in Myrtle Beach, South Carolina in 1981 when they opened for Judas Priest. Iron Maiden's first album and Priest's "Point of Entry" were my absolute favorite albums that year, so seeing them both on one bill was amazing. Honestly, though, I was 15 and go plastered, so I don't remember a whole lot about the show, except that I was blown away at the time. Iron Maiden and Judas Priest remained my two favorite bands all throughout the 80s. I saw Iron Maiden 3 or 4 times with Dickinson in the 80s in Charlotte, North Carolina (and they always obliged us with "Charlotte The Harlot". To this day I'm astonished that Iron Maiden still aren't getting the respect they deserve. But that's one thing that makes them great. They've never needed it. For 50 years Iron Maiden and their fans have had their own thing going outside of the popular mainstream, and I think that's suited both just fine. I'm psyched about this documentary. Feels like it's been a long time coming.

comment: I have always loved IFC Films. I love that its not all big movie companies and is made up of independent film creators, writers, directors, and producers. And that they come from all over the world ! I like that the actors aren't always the ones that have oversaturated the market and aren't super famous, and when ones that are, read a script and are so taken with it, they want a part ! I think that is a beautiful thing. Ireland has had some really great movies come out lately. Thank you IFC. For producing independent films. I prefer your films over all others ! Please keep bringing the world the films that you do. They are often a million times better than the junk, and endless sequels to movies that did well, that the bog movie producers want to conitnue to milk for dollars, but are usually never as good as the original film they made. I find that vulgar, to do that, and boring. You all may not always get awards and the accolades you deserve for what you put out, but that's because the entire system is so heavily biased and bought and paid for by these big movie producers, that youve got little chance against a film, where theyve got buddies on the board, or are benfitting in some way. But you surely deserve more awards and recognition than most films that are put out by the big ones. I hope there is always an IFC, for people who make movies, that people want to see, isntead of what a company can make off the garbage they are producing these days. So......THANK YOU THANK YOU THANK YOU !!!! God bless you and keep up the great work !!!! ✝️🧡🙏🏻

title: Girls Like Girls
comment: I just want to say thank you for bringing this film to life. When the Girls Like Girls music video came out in 2015, it made me feel seen in a way I hadn’t before. Watching it over and over, quietly and privately— felt like having a safe place I could return to whenever I needed it. I’m still not out but it makes me laugh because my siblings basically know the song by heart. I played it like my life depended on it. Seeing the story turn into a film was such a beautiful surprise. I had no idea Hayley was creating something like this for us. Thank you so much for your vision, Hayley. I hope you’re surrounded by the same love you’ve given to so many of us, always.
